thi15:1.0.402: In Pāṭaliputta, the cream of the world,the city named for a flower,there were two nuns from the Sakyan clan,both of them ladies of quality.
thi15:1.0.403: One was named Isidāsī, the second Bodhī.They both were accomplished in ethics,lovers of meditation and chanting,learned, crushing corruptions.
thi15:1.0.404: They wandered for alms and had their meal.When they had washed their bowls,they sat happily in a private placeand started a conversation.
thi15:1.0.405: “You’re so lovely, Venerable Isidāsī,your youth has not yet faded.What problem did you see that made youdedicate your life to renunciation?”
thi15:1.0.406: Being pressed like this in private,Isidāsī, skilled in teaching Dhamma,voiced the following words.“Bodhī, hear how I went forth.
thi15:1.0.407: In the fine town of Ujjenī,my father was a financier, a good and moral man.I was his only daughter,dear, beloved, and cherished.
thi15:1.0.408: Then some suitors came for mefrom the top family of Sāketa.They were sent by a financier abounding in wealth,to whom my father then gave me as daughter-in-law.
thi15:1.0.409: Come morning and come night,I bowed with my head to the feetof my father and mother-in-law,just as I had been told.
thi15:1.0.410: Whenever I saw my husband’s sisters,his brothers, his servants,or even he, my one and only,I nervously gave them a seat.
thi15:1.0.411: Whatever they wanted—food and drink,treats, or whatever was in the cupboard—I brought out and offered to them,ensuring each got what was fitting.
thi15:1.0.412: Having risen bright and early,I approached the main house,washed my hands and feet,and went to my husband with joined palms.
thi15:1.0.413: Taking a comb, adornments,eyeshadow, and a mirror,I myself did the makeup for my husband,as if I were his beautician.
thi15:1.0.414: I myself cooked the rice;I myself washed the pots.I looked after my husbandlike a mother her only child.
thi15:1.0.415: Thus I showed my devotion to him,a loving, virtuous, and humble servant,getting up early, and working tirelessly:yet still my husband did me wrong.
thi15:1.0.416: He said to his mother and father:‘I’ll take my leave and go,I can’t stand to live together with Isidāsīstaying in the same house.’
thi15:1.0.417: ‘Son, don’t speak like this!Isidāsī is astute and competent,she gets up early and works tirelessly,son, why doesn’t she please you?’
thi15:1.0.418: ‘She hasn’t done anything to hurt me,but I just can’t stand to live with her.As far as I’m concerned, she’s just horrible.I’ve had enough, I’ll take my leave and go.’
thi15:1.0.419: When they heard his words,my father-in-law and mother-in-law asked me:‘What did you do wrong?Tell us honestly, have no fear.’
thi15:1.0.420: ‘I’ve done nothing wrong,I haven’t hurt him, or said anything bad.What can I possibly do,when my husband finds me so hateful?’
thi15:1.0.421: They led me back to my father’s home,distraught, overcome with suffering, and said:‘By caring for our son,we’ve lost her, so lovely and lucky!’
thi15:1.0.422: Next my dad gave me to the householdof a second wealthy family-man.For this he got half the bride-priceof that which the financier paid.
thi15:1.0.423: In his house I also lived a month,before he too wanted me gone;though I served him like a slave,virtuous and doing no wrong.
thi15:1.0.424: My father then spoke to a beggar for alms,a tamer of others and of himself:‘Be my son-in-law;set aside your rags and bowl.’
thi15:1.0.425: He stayed a fortnight before he said to my dad:‘Give me back my rag robes,my bowl, and my cup—I’ll wander begging for alms again.’
thi15:1.0.426: So then my mum and my dadand my whole group of relatives said:‘What has not been done for you here?Quickly, tell us what we can do for you!’
thi15:1.0.427: When they spoke to him like this he said,‘If I can make do for myself, that is enough.I can’t stand to live together with Isidāsīstaying in the same house.’
thi15:1.0.428: Released, he left.But I sat all alone contemplating:‘Having taken my leave, I’ll go,either to die or to go forth.’
thi15:1.0.429: But then the venerable lady Jinadattā,learned and virtuous,who had memorized the monastic law,came to my dad’s house in search of alms.
thi15:1.0.430: When I saw her,I got up from my seat and prepared it for her.When she had taken her seat,I honored her feet and offered her a meal,
thi15:1.0.431: satisfying her with food and drink,treats, or whatever was in the cupboard.Then I said:‘Ma’am, I wish to go forth!’
thi15:1.0.432: But my dad said to me:‘Child, practice Dhamma right here!Satisfy ascetics and twice-born brahminswith food and drink.’
thi15:1.0.433: Then I said to my dad,crying, my joined palms raised to him:‘I’ve done bad things in the past;I shall wear that bad deed away.’
thi15:1.0.434: And my dad said to me:‘May you attain awakening, the highest state,and may you find the extinguishmentthat was realized by the best of men!’
thi15:1.0.435: I bowed down to my mother and father,and my whole group of relatives;and then, seven days after going forth,I realized the three knowledges.
thi15:1.0.436: I know my last seven lives;I shall relate to you the deedsof which this life is the fruit and result:focus your whole mind on that.
thi15:1.0.437: In the city of ErakaccaI was a goldsmith with lots of money.Drunk on the pride of youth,I had sex with someone else’s wife.
thi15:1.0.438: Having passed away from there,I burned in hell for a long time.Rising up from thereI was conceived in a monkey’s womb.
thi15:1.0.439: When I was only seven days old,I was castrated by the monkey chief.This was the fruit of that deed,because of adultery with another’s wife.
thi15:1.0.440: Having passed away from there,passing away in Sindhava grove,I was conceived in the wombof a lame, one-eyed she-goat.
thi15:1.0.441: I carried children on my back for twelve years,and all the while I was castrated,worm-eaten, and tail-less,because of adultery with another’s wife.
thi15:1.0.442: Having passed away from there,I was reborn in a cowowned by a cattle merchant.A red calf, castrated, for twelve months
thi15:1.0.443: I drew a big plow.I shouldered a cart,blind, tail-less, feeble,because of adultery with another’s wife.
thi15:1.0.444: Having passed away from there,I was born of a slave in the street,with neither male nor female parts,because of adultery with another’s wife.
thi15:1.0.445: I died at thirty years of age,and was reborn as a girl in a carter’s family.We were poor, of little wealth,greatly oppressed by creditors.
thi15:1.0.446: Because of the huge interest we owed,I was dragged away screaming,taken by force from the family homeby a caravan leader.
thi15:1.0.447: When I was sixteen years old,his son named Giridāsa,seeing that I was a girl of marriageable age,took me as his wife.
thi15:1.0.448: He also had another wife,a virtuous and well-known lady of quality,faithful to her husband;yet I stirred up resentment in her.
thi15:1.0.449: As the fruit of that deed,they abandoned me and left,though I served them like a slave.Now I’ve made an end to this as well.”
